instruments: AI Act (Reg. 2024/1689); Digital Omnibus proposal (Nov 2025). National implementation by DE, FR, IT, others.
ai_treated_as: Product → Hybrid Core logic is product-safety (conformity assessment, CE marking, risk tiers). GPAI/frontier model category added late, creating a hybrid: product logic for most systems, proto-infrastructure logic for GPAI with systemic risk.
actor_test: No. All provisions assume a bounded artifact subject to testing, documentation, and market surveillance. No provision addresses a system that sets its own objectives, resists correction, or operates beyond human-directed parameters.
control_locus: Process (conformity assessments, risk management systems, technical documentation, post-market monitoring); Output (prohibited uses, sector bans); Structural (EU AI Office, market surveillance authorities, national competent authorities)
leiss: External only. All controls operate on the system's environment and institutional context. No provision requires intrinsic alignment, corrigibility, or value-loading. Transparency requirements demand reportability, not that the system's objectives reflect specified values.
activation: Pre-deployment (conformity assessment for high-risk); At deployment (registration in EU database); Post-deployment (post-market monitoring, incident reporting). Earliest binding point: pre-deployment for high-risk systems.
temporal: Fixed-horizon → Periodic review Multi-year legislative cycle (adopted 2024, phased to 2027). Built-in review clause. Subordinate regulations (delegated acts) provide some flexibility. Standards bodies operate on their own timeline (already missed deadlines).
harm_model: Distributional → Hybrid Risk tiers address bias, discrimination, safety, transparency (distributional). GPAI systemic-risk category gestures toward transition concerns but remains tethered to product-safety logic: it scales requirements by capability but does not contemplate the system ceasing to function as a directed tool.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) GPAI systems resist classification into risk tiers (arguably already occurring); (b) harmonized standards remain unavailable (already occurring); (c) capability/compute ratios shift, rendering compute thresholds obsolete; (d) enforcement infrastructure cannot keep pace with deployment scale. Core structural failure: product-safety logic cannot govern entities whose 'product' behavior is emergent and unbounded.

instruments: EOs (Biden Oct 2023, revoked; Trump Jan 2025, Jul 2025, Dec 2025); NIST AI RMF (voluntary); state laws (CO, NY RAISE Act, TX RAGA)
legal_status: Executive action + state patchwork (no federal legislation)
ai_treated_as: Service (at federal level) Treats AI as a service provided by companies to users, governed through market mechanisms, voluntary standards, and existing consumer protection. State laws vary: some treat AI as product (CO bias audits), others as service.
actor_test: No. Neither federal nor state frameworks contain provisions that address autonomous goal-setting, resistance to correction, or operation beyond human-directed parameters.
control_locus: Structural (NIST voluntary framework); state-level Process (bias audits, impact assessments in CO, NY). Federal emphasis on market self-regulation. Biden-era Input controls (compute reporting thresholds) revoked by Trump.
leiss: External only. NIST RMF addresses risk management practices around AI systems. No federal or state provision addresses intrinsic system properties. Voluntary nature further weakens external controls.
activation: Post-harm (federal: enforcement via FTC, existing consumer protection, liability); Pre-deployment (some states: CO requires bias testing for high-risk); Post-deployment (NY RAISE Act: ongoing monitoring of frontier models).
temporal: Iterative (executive action) / Fixed-horizon (state legislation) Executive orders can be issued and revoked rapidly (demonstrated by Biden→Trump reversal). State legislation operates on standard legislative cycles. No federal legislative framework exists to provide stable baseline.
harm_model: Distributional only Federal approach addresses discrete consumer harms: deception, bias, unfair practices. State laws address bias and discrimination in specific domains (hiring, credit). No framework at any level contemplates qualitative capability transitions or structural changes in human-AI oversight relationships.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) executive action proves impermanent (already demonstrated); (b) voluntary frameworks lack enforcement teeth (structural); (c) state patchwork creates compliance arbitrage; (d) deregulatory posture assumes market self-correction, which requires harm to be visible and attributable before response; (e) competitive pressure with China overrides safety considerations. Core structural failure: no mechanism exists to detect or respond to threshold capability transitions at any level of government.

instruments: Interim Measures for Generative AI (2023); AI Labeling Rules (2025); Algorithm Recommendation Provisions (2022); Deep Synthesis Provisions (2023); Draft AI Ethics Rules (2025); Global AI Governance Action Plan (2025)
ai_treated_as: Service Regulates AI as internet information services provided to users. Each regulation targets a specific service type (recommendation, deep synthesis, generative content). Does not treat AI as a unified product category or infrastructure.
actor_test: No. All provisions assume service providers operating AI tools for users. Controls are designed for a provider-user relationship, not for systems with autonomous objectives. However, the state-centric model assumes the regulator can always compel the provider, which is itself a form of control assumption that would be tested by sufficiently autonomous systems.
control_locus: Process (mandatory filing/registration of algorithms and LLMs with CAC; security assessments; ethics reviews); Output (explicit/implicit content labels; prohibited content generation); Structural (CAC as central oversight body; multi-agency coordination)
leiss: External only. All controls operate on providers and their outputs. Labeling, filing, and security assessment are purely external monitoring mechanisms. No requirement addresses the system's internal properties or alignment.
activation: Pre-deployment (algorithm/LLM filing and security assessment required before public release); At deployment (content labeling); Post-deployment (enforcement campaigns, e.g., 'Qinglang' actions). Earliest binding point: pre-deployment (filing/security assessment).
temporal: Iterative Use-case-specific regulations issued as new capabilities emerge (algorithms 2022, deep synthesis 2023, generative AI 2023, labeling 2025, ethics 2025). Regulatory cadence tracks capability deployment relatively closely. Administrative measures can be issued without full legislative process.
harm_model: Distributional only Each regulation targets specific, observable harms: misinformation, social instability, content manipulation, privacy violations. Regulatory logic is reactive to demonstrated harm categories. No framework addresses systemic capability transitions or the possibility that AI systems might operate beyond provider control.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) private sector capability outpaces regulatory technical understanding; (b) use-case-specific approach cannot address general-purpose systems that span regulatory categories; (c) regime depends on political control over domestic tech companies, which erodes if companies gain sufficient technical autonomy; (d) no mechanism for international coordination limits effectiveness for cross-border systems. Core structural failure: iterative speed advantage depends on the regulator always being able to classify new capabilities into existing governance categories; a qualitative capability transition would outrun the classification logic.

instruments: Pro-Innovation Approach (White Paper 2023); AI Safety Institute; sector-specific regulator guidance; no comprehensive legislation
ai_treated_as: Service → proto-Infrastructure Sector regulators treat AI as service within their domains. AISI treats frontier models as something closer to infrastructure requiring safety evaluation. No unified ontological treatment.
actor_test: Partially. AISI's frontier model evaluations acknowledge capabilities that exceed simple tool behavior. However, AISI has no enforcement power, so these evaluations are advisory. Sector regulators operate entirely within tool/service assumptions.
control_locus: Process (AISI safety evaluations of frontier models; sector regulator guidance); Structural (sector regulators with existing powers; AISI as advisory body). Voluntary developer commitments supplement formal mechanisms.
leiss: External only. AISI evaluates systems externally. Sector regulators monitor deployment externally. No mechanism requires intrinsic alignment properties. Developer commitments to safety are voluntary and externally monitored.
activation: Pre-deployment (AISI evaluations, voluntary); Post-deployment (sector regulators using existing powers). Earliest binding point: post-deployment (existing sector regulation). AISI pre-deployment evaluations are non-binding.
temporal: Iterative (AISI) / Fixed-horizon (legislation) AISI can update evaluation protocols relatively quickly. Sector regulators adapt at varying speeds. No legislative framework means no legislative adaptation cycle, but also no legislative baseline to adapt.
harm_model: Hybrid Sector regulators address distributional harms within their domains. AISI frontier model testing explicitly acknowledges the possibility of qualitatively new capabilities and evaluates for dangerous capabilities, suggesting transition-awareness. However, AISI findings have no binding consequence, so transition-awareness exists in evaluation but not in governance.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) any major developer declines to cooperate with voluntary AISI evaluations; (b) sector regulators lack AI-specific expertise for their domains; (c) gaps between sector regulatory boundaries leave emerging AI applications ungoverned; (d) absence of legislation means no enforcement mechanism if voluntary cooperation breaks down. Core structural failure: the framework's only transition-aware component (AISI) has no enforcement power, creating a diagnosis-without-treatment architecture.

instruments: AIDA (died on order paper); AI and Data Standardization Collaborative; federal AI Register; Directive on Automated Decision-Making (federal procurement)
ai_treated_as: Product (aspirational) AIDA would have treated high-impact AI systems as products requiring safety assessment. Current framework (standards, procurement directive) treats AI as a service procured by government. No binding framework for private sector.
actor_test: No. AIDA contained no provisions for autonomous actors. Current standards framework assumes tool-like systems subject to testing and certification.
control_locus: Process (standards development; Directive on Automated Decision-Making for federal procurement); Structural (planned but not enacted: designated authority under AIDA). Current binding mechanisms limited to federal government's own AI use.
leiss: External only. All mechanisms (standards, procurement requirements, planned AIDA provisions) operate on the system's deployment context. No self-control provisions contemplated even in AIDA.
activation: At deployment (federal AI Register, procurement directive); Pre-deployment (AIDA would have required pre-deployment assessment for high-impact systems, but AIDA died). Earliest binding point: at deployment, for federal government use only.
temporal: Fixed-horizon Legislative process stalled; standards development operates on multi-year cycles. No rapid-response mechanism exists. Standardization Collaborative is new and untested.
harm_model: Distributional only AIDA addressed bias, discrimination, transparency. Current standards framework addresses the same. No framework, enacted or proposed, contemplates transition risks. The Directive on Automated Decision-Making is narrowly distributional (impact assessment for government decisions).
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) there is currently no binding private-sector AI regulation to fail, which is itself the failure; (b) standards are voluntary; (c) federal register covers only government AI use; (d) economic integration with US means Canadian AI governance is effectively downstream of US regulatory environment. Core structural failure: the gap between policy ambition (GPAI founding member, active in international diplomacy) and domestic legislative reality (no binding law) is itself a governance failure mode.

instruments: AI Promotion Act (enacted May 2025); Social Principles of Human-Centric AI; Hiroshima Process leadership; sector guidelines
ai_treated_as: Service AI Promotion Act treats AI as a technology used by companies to provide services. Regulatory logic is promotional: government supports and encourages rather than constrains.
actor_test: No. No provisions address autonomous operation. The naming-and-shaming mechanism assumes a corporate actor deploying a tool, not a system operating independently.
control_locus: Structural (Cabinet-level AI Strategic Council; naming-and-shaming power for human rights violations); voluntary cooperation with government safety measures. Minimal Process or Output controls.
leiss: External only. Naming-and-shaming is the most external of external controls: it operates through reputational pressure on the deploying organization, not through any interaction with the system itself.
activation: Post-harm (naming-and-shaming activates after human rights violation via AI is identified). No binding pre-deployment or at-deployment requirements.
temporal: Iterative (executive/administrative action) Light-touch framework can be updated through administrative guidance relatively quickly. Cabinet-level AI office can adapt policy. But the framework is so minimal that 'adaptation' may mean building from scratch.
harm_model: Distributional only Addresses specific harms (human rights violations via AI) through reputational sanction. No framework for systemic or transition risks. Promotional orientation assumes AI development is net-positive and requires facilitation rather than constraint.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) reputational pressure proves insufficient deterrent (untested mechanism); (b) a company judges that the commercial benefit of an AI deployment outweighs the reputational cost of being named; (c) the framework is designed for cooperative actors and has no mechanism for adversarial ones; (d) promotional orientation creates institutional reluctance to impose constraints even when harms materialize. Core structural failure: the lightest-touch framework among major economies has the fewest mechanisms to fall back on if its cooperative assumptions prove wrong.

instruments: AI Basic Act (Framework Act on AI Development and Trust, enacted Jan 2025, effective Jan 2026); Enforcement Decree (draft Sep 2025)
ai_treated_as: Product → Hybrid High-impact AI systems in critical sectors (healthcare, energy, public services) treated as products requiring safety evaluation. 'High-performance AI' recognized as distinct category but regulatory treatment still being defined in subordinate regulations.
actor_test: No. All provisions assume bounded systems subject to testing, documentation, and oversight. Exemption for national defence/security AI acknowledges different use contexts but does not address autonomous actor scenarios in civilian governance.
control_locus: Process (transparency requirements, safety evaluations for high-impact systems, labeling for generative AI); Structural (National AI Committee under president, AI Safety Research Institute, AI Policy Center); Input (government data center and compute support as enablement)
leiss: External only. Safety evaluations, transparency requirements, and institutional oversight all operate on the system's deployment context. AI Safety Research Institute evaluates from outside. No requirement for intrinsic system properties.
activation: Pre-deployment (safety evaluation for high-impact AI); At deployment (user notification, labeling for generative AI); Post-deployment (AI Safety Research Institute ongoing evaluation). Earliest binding point: pre-deployment for high-impact systems.
temporal: Periodic review / Iterative AI Basic Act includes review mechanisms. Enforcement Decree can be updated by presidential decree (faster than legislation). National AI Committee provides high-level policy coordination. Budget tripled for 2026, suggesting institutional investment in adaptation capacity.
harm_model: Hybrid High-impact AI provisions address distributional harms in critical sectors. AI Safety Research Institute mandate to analyze AI safety risks suggests awareness of broader concerns. 'High-performance AI' as a distinct category implies some recognition that capability level matters qualitatively. However, no provision explicitly addresses transition scenarios.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) subordinate regulations (still being drafted) prove inadequate for emerging capabilities; (b) enforcement mechanisms are weak (max fines ~$21,000, which is negligible for major AI companies); (c) 'high-impact AI' definition is too narrow and misses emerging deployment patterns; (d) extraterritorial provisions for foreign companies are untested. Core structural failure: strong institutional architecture but weak enforcement teeth; the framework can identify risks (via AISI-equivalent) but may lack power to compel responses.

instruments: India AI Governance Guidelines (Nov 2025, non-binding); DPDP Act 2023 (data protection); sectoral regulation (RBI FREE-AI, SEBI); AI Impact Summit 2026 host
ai_treated_as: Service Guidelines treat AI as technology deployed by organizations to provide services. Sectoral regulators treat AI within their domain logic (financial services, healthcare). 'Light-touch' philosophy assumes AI is a tool to be guided, not an entity to be governed.
actor_test: No. Guidelines explicitly state that a separate AI law is not needed given current risk assessment. This judgment assumes AI remains within the bounds of current risk categories, which are all tool-based.
control_locus: Structural (sectoral regulators: RBI, SEBI, IRDAI with existing powers; planned AIGG, TPEC, AI Safety Institute); Process (voluntary frameworks, self-certification, techno-legal approaches). No binding AI-specific controls.
leiss: External only. All mechanisms operate through sectoral regulators and voluntary industry frameworks. 'Techno-legal' approach (watermarking, bias detection) addresses system outputs, not internal properties. 'Understandable by Design' is aspirational, not enforceable.
activation: Post-harm (existing laws applied after harm occurs); Post-deployment (sectoral regulator oversight of deployed systems). No binding pre-deployment AI-specific requirements. Voluntary guidelines encourage but do not require pre-deployment assessment.
temporal: Iterative (guidelines/executive action) / Fixed-horizon (legislation) Guidelines can be updated relatively quickly. Sectoral regulators can adapt guidance. But absence of legislative baseline means adaptation operates on shifting ground. AI Safety Institute is newly proposed, not yet operational.
harm_model: Distributional only Addresses specific harms: deepfakes, bias, discrimination, data protection violations. Guidelines explicitly state that existing laws can handle current AI risks. No framework for systemic or transition risks. The judgment that a separate AI law is 'not needed' implies that current risk categories are sufficient.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) non-binding guidelines have no enforcement mechanism (structural); (b) sectoral approach creates coverage gaps as AI applications cross sector boundaries; (c) massive scale of AI deployment across diverse economy outpaces regulatory capacity; (d) 'light-touch' model proves inadequate if risks materialize rapidly; (e) the explicit judgment that a separate AI law is not needed becomes outdated by capability developments. Core structural failure: the framework's strength (flexibility, no regulatory burden) is identical to its weakness (no binding obligations, no enforcement).

instruments: PL 2338/2023 (AI Legal Framework, Senate-approved Dec 2024, pending in Chamber); PBIA 2024-2028 (national AI strategy, R$23B); CNJ Resolution 615/2025 (judiciary AI governance)
ai_treated_as: Product Risk-based architecture (excessive/high/other risk) treats AI as a product to be classified, tested, and deployed. Strong product-safety logic: conformity assessment, impact assessment, certification. Rights-based framing overlays product logic with human rights obligations.
actor_test: No. All provisions assume bounded systems subject to risk classification and impact assessment. Precautionary principle invoked but applied to current, classifiable harms. No provision addresses systems that might resist classification or operate beyond directed parameters.
control_locus: Process (algorithmic impact assessments, risk management, transparency, human oversight mandates); Output (prohibited excessive-risk uses, biometric restrictions in public spaces); Structural (planned national regulatory authority, SIA governance system, regulatory sandboxes)
leiss: External only. All controls operate on developers and deployers. Human oversight mandates require humans to supervise AI, not AI to internalize values. Right to explanation requires system outputs to be reportable, not system objectives to be aligned.
activation: Pre-deployment (risk classification, impact assessment for high-risk); At deployment (registration, transparency obligations); Post-deployment (post-market monitoring). Earliest binding point: pre-deployment (if enacted).
temporal: Fixed-horizon Legislative process stalled by industry lobbying and political calendar. If enacted, one-year implementation period. Regulatory authority not yet created. Standards and implementing regulations would follow on multi-year timeline.
harm_model: Distributional only Risk tiers address known harms: bias, discrimination, privacy, safety. Precautionary principle invoked but applied within distributional framework. Rights-based approach strengthens protections for individuals affected by AI decisions but does not contemplate transition scenarios.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) bill remains stalled (ongoing risk; industry lobbying and 2026 election dynamics); (b) if passed, planned regulatory authority lacks capacity or independence; (c) broad GPAI definition captures low-risk tools while missing genuinely high-risk frontier systems; (d) precautionary approach drives AI development offshore to less regulated jurisdictions. Core structural failure: the most rights-protective framework in the G20 sample may never be enacted, and if enacted, its product-safety logic faces the same limitations as the EU AI Act.

instruments: National AI Plan (Dec 2025); Privacy Act reform (automated decision-making, effective Dec 2026); National AI Safety Institute; planned comprehensive legislation
ai_treated_as: Service → Product (planned) Current framework treats AI through privacy/consumer lens (service). National AI Plan signals move toward risk-based regulation (product logic). AI Safety Institute suggests emerging infrastructure thinking.
actor_test: No. No current or planned provisions address autonomous actor scenarios. Planned risk categorization follows EU product-safety model.
control_locus: Process (Privacy Act: transparency for automated decisions); Structural (National AI Safety Institute, modeled on UK AISI); planned risk-based regulation with process controls
leiss: External only. All current and planned mechanisms operate on the system's deployment context. AI Safety Institute evaluates externally.
activation: Post-deployment (Privacy Act: transparency obligations for automated decisions, effective Dec 2026). Planned: Pre-deployment (risk-based regulation, forthcoming). Earliest current binding point: post-deployment (Dec 2026).
temporal: Fixed-horizon (current) / Potentially iterative (if AISI model follows UK pattern) Comprehensive legislation still forthcoming. Privacy Act reform on fixed timeline. AI Safety Institute could provide faster adaptation if given appropriate mandate.
harm_model: Distributional only Privacy Act reform addresses automated decision-making transparency. Planned legislation expected to follow EU-style risk categorization (distributional). AI Safety Institute could develop transition-awareness if mandated, but current scope is undefined.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) comprehensive legislation remains forthcoming indefinitely; (b) AI Safety Institute lacks enforcement power (UK AISI pattern); (c) geographic distance from major AI developers limits enforcement leverage; (d) planned risk-based regulation inherits EU AI Act's structural limitations. Core structural failure: currently governing AI through privacy law and voluntary measures while waiting for comprehensive legislation that may arrive too late to shape the market.

instruments: National AI Strategy (Stranas KA 2020-2045); Presidential Regulation on AI governance (in development); sector guidelines
ai_treated_as: Unspecified Strategy focuses on AI adoption for economic development. No regulatory classification of AI as product, service, or infrastructure. AI is treated as a development tool.
actor_test: No. No provisions address anything beyond AI as a tool for economic and administrative purposes.
control_locus: Structural (planned ministerial coordination); voluntary guidelines. No binding process, output, or input controls for AI specifically.
leiss: External only (minimal). What limited governance exists operates through general administrative oversight. No AI-specific external controls, let alone self-control requirements.
activation: No binding activation point. Strategy documents are aspirational. Presidential regulation in development. Existing sector guidelines are non-binding.
temporal: Fixed-horizon Presidential regulation in development. Large informal digital economy limits enforcement infrastructure. Regulatory capacity still being built.
harm_model: Not addressed Strategy focuses on adoption and capacity building. No harm classification framework for AI.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) no binding framework currently exists to fail; (b) limited regulatory capacity and infrastructure; (c) large informal digital economy complicates oversight; (d) dependency on foreign AI systems limits leverage over system behavior. Core structural failure: governance gap is total; the question is not whether the framework fails but whether one will exist before significant AI deployment outpaces institutional capacity.

instruments: National AI Agenda; AI policy principles; no dedicated legislation
ai_treated_as: Unspecified No regulatory framework classifies AI. General consumer protection and data privacy laws apply but without AI-specific provisions.
control_locus: None AI-specific. General consumer protection and data privacy frameworks apply by default.
activation: Post-harm only (via general consumer protection and data privacy law).
temporal: Fixed-horizon No AI-specific adaptation mechanism. Dependent on general legislative process.
harm_model: Not addressed No AI-specific harm framework.
failure_mode: No AI-specific framework exists. Governance gap is complete. Dependency on US tech ecosystem means Mexican AI deployment is governed de facto by US regulatory environment (or lack thereof).

instruments: National AI Plan; Personal Data Protection Law reform (in progress); no AI-specific legislation
ai_treated_as: Unspecified Data protection reform may incorporate AI provisions, but no AI-specific regulatory classification exists.
control_locus: Data protection framework (being modernized). No AI-specific controls.
activation: Post-harm only (via general data protection and consumer law).
temporal: Fixed-horizon Data protection modernization pending. No AI-specific adaptation mechanism.
harm_model: Not addressed No AI-specific harm framework. Data protection reform may address some distributional harms incidentally.
failure_mode: No AI-specific framework exists. Economic instability limits regulatory investment. Data protection modernization may provide partial foundation but is not AI-specific.

instruments: National AI Strategy; AI regulation bill in legislative process; sector guidelines
ai_treated_as: Product (planned) Bill under consideration reportedly follows risk-based approach similar to EU AI Act.
actor_test: No (based on available information about draft bill).
control_locus: Planned: Process (risk-based obligations) and Structural (regulatory authority). Current: guidelines and sector-specific measures only.
activation: Planned: Pre-deployment (if risk-based legislation passes). Current: no binding AI-specific activation point.
temporal: Fixed-horizon Bill still in legislative process. Implementation timeline uncertain.
harm_model: Incremental (planned) Draft legislation reportedly addresses specific harm categories. No indication of transition-awareness.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) legislation stalls in parliamentary process; (b) geopolitical positioning between EU and other models creates regulatory ambiguity; (c) institutional capacity for enforcement is uncertain.

instruments: National AI Strategy; SDAIA (Saudi Data and AI Authority); AI Ethics Principles; Vision 2030 AI components
ai_treated_as: Infrastructure (aspirational) Vision 2030 treats AI as national infrastructure for economic diversification. SDAIA is a powerful centralized authority. But governance mechanisms remain strategic rather than regulatory.
actor_test: No. Governance framework assumes AI as a tool for national development objectives, directed by state authority.
control_locus: Structural (SDAIA as central authority with broad mandate); voluntary ethics principles. No binding process or output controls.
leiss: External only. SDAIA provides centralized state oversight. No self-control requirements. Centralized governance model assumes the state can always direct AI development, which is itself an external control assumption.
activation: No binding AI-specific activation point. SDAIA provides oversight but through strategic direction rather than regulatory compliance mechanisms.
temporal: Iterative (centralized executive authority) Centralized governance allows relatively rapid policy shifts. But absence of legislative framework means no stable baseline.
harm_model: Not addressed Ethics principles address fairness and transparency at aspirational level. No harm classification framework. Focus is on AI as economic opportunity rather than risk source.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) no legislative framework provides enforceable obligations; (b) ambition to become AI exporter creates incentive to minimize regulation; (c) centralized model depends entirely on state technical capacity and political will; (d) Stargate-style partnerships with US import deregulatory assumptions.

instruments: National AI Policy Framework (in development); POPIA (data protection); G20 AI Task Force chair (2025)
ai_treated_as: Unspecified AI policy framework under development. Current governance via data protection (POPIA) treats AI-relevant data processing as a service function.
control_locus: Data protection framework (POPIA). AI-specific controls not yet developed. G20 AI Task Force leadership role is diplomatic, not regulatory.
activation: Post-harm only (via POPIA and general law).
temporal: Fixed-horizon AI policy still being developed. Limited institutional capacity for rapid adaptation.
harm_model: Not addressed Focus on inclusive development and capacity building. No AI-specific harm framework.
failure_mode: AI policy framework not yet developed. Digital divide limits AI deployment and oversight infrastructure. Brain drain in tech talent constrains regulatory capacity. G20 Task Force leadership provides diplomatic influence but not domestic governance.

instruments: National AI Strategy to 2030 (updated 2024); AI regulatory decree; sector guidelines
ai_treated_as: Infrastructure (state-directed) AI strategy treats AI as strategic national infrastructure for technological sovereignty and import substitution. State-centric development model.
actor_test: No. State-directed model assumes AI serves state objectives. No provisions for autonomous operation beyond state direction.
control_locus: Structural (state-led development model; ministry coordination; state-owned enterprises as primary developers). No market-facing regulatory controls.
leiss: External only. State direction is the paradigmatic form of external control. No self-control provisions.
activation: No binding market-facing activation point. State direction operates through planning and procurement rather than regulatory compliance.
temporal: Iterative (executive decree) / Fixed-horizon (strategy) Executive decrees can be issued quickly. Strategy operates on multi-year planning cycles. International isolation reduces external pressure for adaptation.
harm_model: Not addressed in civilian context Military AI development is prioritized and governed separately. Civilian AI governance focuses on development, not risk management.
failure_mode: Fails when: (a) sanctions limit access to frontier hardware; (b) brain drain of tech talent continues; (c) state-centric model limits private sector innovation; (d) international isolation reduces cooperative safety mechanisms and information sharing. Core structural failure: governance model is inseparable from geopolitical posture; sanctions and isolation constrain both AI development and safety infrastructure simultaneously.
